behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels.
behaviors.justification: The failure is in the module's payload construction code—missing or incomplete labels in the request body. We can verify this by mocking the HTTP client and inspecting call arguments to check the payload structure. No external I/O or real API is required to catch a malformed request.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the HTTP client. Call the classification module with a sample document. Inspect the mocked HTTP call's request payload and verify it includes all valid taxonomy labels (e.g., 'legal', 'technical', 'financial', 'other').
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit-level inspection of request arguments catches payload construction bugs reliably. The failure point is local code, not I/O.

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy, the module returns 'unknown' instead of forwarding the unexpected value to the caller.
behaviors.justification: Response validation is local business logic. We can test it by mocking the API to return an invalid label and verifying the module's validation code returns 'unknown' instead of the unexpected value. No I/O layer is required to verify this logic.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the API to return a label outside the configured taxonomy (e.g., 'fraudulent_classification' when only 'legal', 'technical', 'financial', 'other' are valid). Call the module and verify it returns 'unknown' rather than propagating the invalid label.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit test directly exercises the response validation logic. A mock returning an invalid label will reliably trigger the code path.

behaviors.description: The classification result for the same document is the same across repeated calls.
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests result caching to avoid redundant API calls and control costs. We mock the API, call the module twice with the same input, and verify the API is invoked only once (proving caching). Using a fake clock ensures the cache key does not depend on time-based invalidation.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the API and use a fake clock or time freezing. Call the module twice with identical document input. Verify the mocked API is called only once and both module calls return the same cached result.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit test with fake time controls detects missing caching and time-dependent cache key bugs. The failure (no caching or cache invalidation on each call) manifests as the API being called more than once.

behaviors.description: The number of tokens consumed for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget.
behaviors.justification: Token counting accuracy is critical—mocking the token counter would hide real bugs in token measurement. We must use a real token counter (e.g., tiktoken) to verify the module respects the budget. Mocking the external API is acceptable, but not the token counting layer.
behaviors.test_description: Use a real token counter library (e.g., tiktoken). Mock the external API. Create a typical document (e.g., 500 tokens), configure a per-call budget (e.g., 1000 tokens), call the module, and verify the tokens consumed do not exceed the configured budget.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration-level test with real token counter catches budget enforcement bugs and token counting inaccuracies. A pure mock token counter would fail to detect mismatches between real API token counts and our estimates.

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high, the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller.
behaviors.justification: Retry logic and backoff are implemented as local code in the module. We can verify this by mocking the API to return a 429 status, mocking the time.sleep function, and confirming the module retries instead of propagating an error. No real network or external I/O is required.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the API to return HTTP 429 on the first call and a valid 200 response on the second call. Mock the time.sleep function. Call the module and verify it waits and retries, ultimately returning the valid classification result instead of raising a rate-limit error.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit test with mocked time and API directly exercises retry logic. A mock returning 429 reliably triggers the retry path without requiring real network conditions.
